narrative: As a Black male applicant with a strong undergraduate GPA of 3.90—well above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in several ways, including your plan to attend full-time like most students and your lower-middle family income background. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 45% chance that you would fail it, tipping the decision toward rejection to minimize the risk of poor bar passage outcomes for the school. This prediction was driven primarily by three key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence on the outcome. Your outstanding undergrad GPA was the strongest positive force, strongly pushing the model toward expecting you to pass the bar, as it signals exceptional academic ability beyond what's typical for applicants. In contrast, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1—far below the average of 5.53—was the biggest drag, heavily influencing the model to predict failure because it highlights underperformance relative to peers in later undergrad years. Your LSAT score of 21, much lower than the average 36.70, also pulled toward a failure prediction, though with less force than your decile rank. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile shows a mixed picture: elite undergrad grades that align with success stories, but weaker later-year school performance and LSAT that mirror patterns among those who struggle. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to average levels, strengthening your Year 3 record through additional coursework or explanations, and targeting schools with holistic reviews that value your high GPA and personal background. With targeted improvements, you can reshape this prediction and find the right path to law school.
